From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

2007 film by Martin Weisz

This article is about the sequel to the 2006 remake. For the sequel to the original 1977 film, see [The Hills Have Eyes Part II](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_Part_II "The Hills Have Eyes Part II").

| The Hills Have Eyes 2 | |

***The Hills Have Eyes 2*** is a 2007 American [horror film](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film") and the sequel to [the 2006 film](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_(2006_film) "The Hills Have Eyes (2006 film)"), which itself was a remake of [the 1977 horror film](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_(1977_film) "The Hills Have Eyes (1977 film)"). The film follows several [New Mexico Army National Guard](/wiki/New_Mexico_Army_National_Guard "New Mexico Army National Guard") comrades as they fight for survival against the [mutant](/wiki/Mutant_(fiction) "Mutant (fiction)") people living in a [military base](/wiki/Military_base "Military base") in the [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") [desert](/wiki/Desert "Desert"). It stars [Michael McMillian](/wiki/Michael_McMillian "Michael McMillian"), [Jacob Vargas](/wiki/Jacob_Vargas "Jacob Vargas"), [Flex Alexander](/wiki/Flex_Alexander "Flex Alexander") and [Jessica Stroup](/wiki/Jessica_Stroup "Jessica Stroup"). *The Hills Have Eyes 2* was directed by German film director [Martin Weisz](/wiki/Martin_Weisz "Martin Weisz"), and written by father and son team [Wes](/wiki/Wes_Craven "Wes Craven") and [Jonathan Craven](/wiki/Jonathan_Craven "Jonathan Craven").[[3]](#cite_note-The_Hills_Have_Eyes_2-3) A [graphic novel](/wiki/Graphic_novel "Graphic novel") titled *[The Hills Have Eyes: The Beginning](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes:_The_Beginning "The Hills Have Eyes: The Beginning")* was published by [Fox Atomic Comics](/wiki/Fox_Atomic_Comics "Fox Atomic Comics") to accompany the release of the film; it was released on July 3, 2007.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Hills_Have_Eyes_2&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

A captive woman, forced to breed [mutant](/wiki/Mutants_in_fiction "Mutants in fiction") children, is unable to provide a healthy offspring after giving birth to a stillborn and is killed by Papa Hades, leader of the [inbred mutant clan living at a nuclear testing village in the New Mexico desert](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_(2006_film)#Plot "The Hills Have Eyes (2006 film)"), designated as Sector 16. [Following the events of the first film](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_(2006_film) "The Hills Have Eyes (2006 film)"), the [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") sent in the military to occupy the location and exterminate the remainder of the mutant clan. While installing a [surveillance](/wiki/Surveillance "Surveillance") system, Colonel Lincoln Redding and a trio of scientists are attacked by the mutants.

Later, a group of National Guardsmen in training led by Sergeant Jeffrey "Sarge" Millstone are sent into the desert to resupply the scientists. They arrive to find the base camp abandoned after the mutants' attack. When the soldiers pick up a faint distress call, Sarge organizes a search and rescue mission through the hills. Napoleon and Amber, who stay behind at the camp, find and retrieve one of the missing scientists out of the camp's [portable toilet](/wiki/Portable_toilet "Portable toilet"), but he dies before he can warn them. The two are then attacked by the mutants, who destroy their transport and steal their weapons, forcing them to run into the hills to warn the others. The search party is attacked by the mutants; Mickey is pulled into a bolt-hole and killed by Stabber, and Sarge is accidentally shot dead by Spitter's friendly fire just before Napoleon and Amber reunite with the group. Spitter is killed after one of the mutants sabotages his [rappelling](/wiki/Rappelling "Rappelling") gear as the others try to lower him down the hill. With their remaining gear stolen, they are forced to try to find another way to escape.

The remaining troops soon locate Redding, mortally wounded and unhinged from the mutant's attack. He warns them of the mutants' plans to capture women for breeding and reveals an exit route through the mining caves before committing suicide. After the group kills Stabber, Missy is captured and taken into the mining caves by Chameleon, forcing the other troops to rescue her. Stump leaves the group to escape on his own before being pursued and killed by Letch while attempting to climb down the hill. Separating from Crank and Delmar, Napoleon and Amber cross paths with Chameleon, whom they manage to kill. While escaping Grabber, they are rescued by a nonviolent mutant named Hansel; Grabber encounters Crank and Delmar, shoots the latter with one of the group's [M4 carbines](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine") and is killed by Crank in retaliation. While Hansel leads them to the exit, Delmar dies from his wounds after arriving at the mine's doorway. Napoleon joins Amber to save Missy. Crank, who is attempting to unlock the [blast door](/wiki/Blast_door "Blast door"), dies after accidentally triggering an explosion of a crate of dynamite that he attempts to take with him.

After killing Letch, Napoleon and Amber locate Missy who is being savagely raped by Papa Hades. After distracting him, they manage to free her, but Hades soon returns and attacks them, triggering a vicious fight until the trio finally subdues and kills the mutant leader with a bayonet. As the survivors prepare to depart the mines, they are watched by an unknown mutant using their surveillance equipment.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Hills_Have_Eyes_2&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

Writer Wes Craven's initial inspiration for the film came during a casual conversation with producer Peter Locke. Craven envisioned that the previous film's character, Brenda ([Emilie de Ravin](/wiki/Emilie_de_Ravin "Emilie de Ravin")), traumatized by her suffering during the events of *[The Hills Have Eyes](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_(2006_film) "The Hills Have Eyes (2006 film)")*, joins the National Guard to overcome her fears. Barely finished with basic training, Brenda receives a call from her sergeant, who explains that they are sending a team back to the New Mexico desert to eradicate the remaining mutants. Her sergeant and the team need her, for she is the only one left alive who knows the mutants' location. Because of de Ravin's involvement in the television show *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)")*, her schedule was unable to accommodate the filming of the sequel. [Wes Craven](/wiki/Wes_Craven "Wes Craven") replaced her character, but retained much of the original concept, including the group of National Guard soldiers in training.[[4]](#cite_note-THHE2-4)

Craven originally looked at [M. J. Bassett](/wiki/M._J._Bassett "M. J. Bassett"), the director of *[Deathwatch](/wiki/Deathwatch_(2002_film) "Deathwatch (2002 film)")*, to take over the directing role, but ultimately chose Martin Weisz after scheduling conflicts with Bassett. *The Hills Have Eyes 2* had a budget of [$](/wiki/USD "USD")15 million,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and began filming in the summer of 2006 in [Ouarzazate](/wiki/Ouarzazate "Ouarzazate"), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), where the previous movie was filmed.

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Hills_Have_Eyes_2&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Release")]

*The Hills Have Eyes 2* was released in theatres on March 23, 2007.[[6]](#cite_note-NYT-6) The film was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") on July 17, 2007, by [20th Century Fox Home Entertainment](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox_Home_Entertainment "20th Century Fox Home Entertainment").[[7]](#cite_note-7) It also grossed over $30 million in domestic DVD sales, for a total of $67,915,885.[[8]](#cite_note-dvd-sales-8)

### Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Hills_Have_Eyes_2&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Marketing")]

A one-minute teaser trailer was released on December 12, 2006. The teaser featured "Insect Eyes", a song by indie folk recording artist [Devendra Banhart](/wiki/Devendra_Banhart "Devendra Banhart").[[9]](#cite_note-9) In addition, the [Fox Atomic](/wiki/Fox_Atomic "Fox Atomic") website released a series of clips with an introduction by [Wes Craven](/wiki/Wes_Craven "Wes Craven").

A [graphic novel](/wiki/Graphic_novel "Graphic novel") titled *[The Hills Have Eyes: The Beginning](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes:_The_Beginning "The Hills Have Eyes: The Beginning")* was published by Fox Atomic Comics to accompany the release of the film; it was released July 3, 2007.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Hills_Have_Eyes_2&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reception")]

The total gross at the [box office](/wiki/Box_office "Box office") of *The Hills Have Eyes 2* was $37.6 million, about half of the original's total gross.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Hills_Have_Eyes_2&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Critical response")]

[Review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") gives the film a score of 13% based on reviews from 64 critics and a rating average of 3.2 out of 10.[[12]](#cite_note-RT-12) The site's critical consensus reads, "*The Hills Have Eyes 2* is a completely unoriginal sequel that offers plenty of gore and clichés, but few scares." *[TV Guide](/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide")* gives the film one out of five stars.[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Film critic](/wiki/Film_criticism "Film criticism") [Peter Bradshaw](/wiki/Peter_Bradshaw "Peter Bradshaw") of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* wrote in his review which was printed in the *[Taipei Times](/wiki/Taipei_Times "Taipei Times")*: "The [sequel](/wiki/Sequel "Sequel") of the [remake](/wiki/Remake "Remake") of [Wes Craven](/wiki/Wes_Craven "Wes Craven")'s *[The Hills Have Eyes](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_(1977_film) "The Hills Have Eyes (1977 film)")* has mutated into a boring mess of a movie."[[14]](#cite_note-Taipei-14) Review aggregator [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which assigns a weighted average score out of 100 to reviews from mainstream critics, the film received an average score of 32% based on 10 reviews.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Critic [Matt Zoller Seitz](/wiki/Matt_Zoller_Seitz "Matt Zoller Seitz") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote in his review: "[Wes Craven](/wiki/Wes_Craven "Wes Craven")'s 1977 film, *The Hills Have Eyes*, in which suburbanites battled mutant cannibals, was a pulpy parable of the thin line separating civilization from savagery. The 2006 remake *[The Hills Have Eyes](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_(2006_film) "The Hills Have Eyes (2006 film)")* was basically the same movie with glossier production values and a less satirical, more bludgeoning approach to violence. This follow-up — in which [National Guard](/wiki/National_Guard_of_the_United_States "National Guard of the United States") trainees are trapped on a former atomic test site and are stalked by flesh-eating freaks headquartered in a warrenlike mountain hideout — is essentially a catalog of transgressive images, lighted and edited like a heavy-metal video."[[6]](#cite_note-NYT-6)

Scott Tobias of *[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")* wrote in his review: "The premise for *The Hills Have Eyes 2*, the quickie follow-up to [Alexandre Aja](/wiki/Alexandre_Aja "Alexandre Aja")'s skillful but gratuitous [2006 remake of Craven's original](/wiki/The_Hills_Have_Eyes_(2006_film) "The Hills Have Eyes (2006 film)"), seems like a perfect opportunity to give the mutants their due, since it deploys a group of military people back to the scene of the crime. And yet it stupidly does the opposite, reducing the mutants to mine-dwelling freaks who murder and rape because, well, that's what they do. After a prologue so repugnant that it's unworthy of description, the film touches down in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico")'s "Sector 15", where a handful of military technicians are busy installing a top-secret surveillance system. When a group of National Guard trainees are dispatched to the site to deliver equipment, they're shocked to discover the men either missing or dead, and they start combing the surrounding hills on a search-and-rescue mission. What they don't realize is that the mutants are luring them into various traps designed to kill the men and abduct the women for (ugh) breeding purposes. So it's up to these unseasoned and often downright inept soldiers to fight their way out of trouble. Directed by music-video veteran [Martin Weisz](/wiki/Martin_Weisz "Martin Weisz")—in the future, can producers please look elsewhere for talent?—*The Hills Have Eyes 2* assembles the most motley group of incompetents this side of a [*Police Academy* movie](/wiki/Police_Academy_(franchise) "Police Academy (franchise)"), yet somehow misses the laughs."[[16]](#cite_note-16)
